title: 1563rd meeting (June 2026) (DH) - Action report (23/03/2026) - Communication from the United Kingdom concerning the case of Neophytou v. the United Kingdom (Application No. 28805/21)  [anglais uniquement)
text: SECRETARIAT / SECRÉTARIAT SECRETARIAT OF THE COMMITTEE OF MINISTERS SECRÉTARIAT DU COMITÉ DES MINISTRES Contact: Ireneusz Kondak Tel: 03.90.21.59.86 Date: 24/03/2026 DH-DD(2026)385 Documents distributed at the request of a Representative shall be under the sole responsibility of the said Representative, without prejudice to the legal or political position of the Committee of Ministers. Meeting: 1563rd meeting (June 2026) (DH) Item reference: Action Report (23/03/2026) Communication from the United Kingdom concerning the case of Neophytou v. the United Kingdom (Application No. 28805/21) * * * * * * * * * * * Les documents distribués à la demande d’un/e Représentant/e le sont sous la seule responsabilité dudit/de ladite Représentant/e, sans préjuger de la position juridique ou politique du Comité des Ministres. Réunion : 1563e réunion (juin 2026) (DH) Référence du point : Bilan d’action (23/03/2026) Communication du Royaume-Uni concernant l’affaire Neophytou c. Royaume-Uni (requête n° 28805/21) (anglais uniquement) DH-DD(2026)385: Communication from the United Kingdom. Documents distributed at the request of a Representative shall be under the sole responsibility of the said DGI Representative, without prejudice to the legal or political position of the Committee of Ministers. 23 MARS 2026 SERVICE DE L’EXECUTION DES ARRETS DE LA CEDH Execution of Judgments of the European Court of Human Rights ACTION REPORT CASE OF NEOPHYTOU V THE UNITED KINGDOM Application number 28805/21 Judgment final on 23 September 2025 Information submitted by the United Kingdom Government on 23 March 2026. CASE SUMMARY 1. The applicants, father and son, Mr Achilleos Neophytou and Mr Stefanos Neophytou were convicted in the Crown Court on 21 September 2016 of conspiring to manage a brothel. On 17 October 2016, they were sentenced to twenty-seven months’ imprisonment and released on licence on 3 December 2017. The prosecution applied for a confiscation order under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 and a directions hearing in relation to the application took place on 19 May 2017, as listed. 2. The ECtHR considered the length of the confiscation proceedings to be excessive, specifically the period between the applicants’ conviction in the Crown Court and the refusal by the Court of Appeal of permission to appeal. The relevant period spanned four years and five months, beginning with the applicants’ conviction on 21 September 2016 and ending with the Court of Appeal’s refusal to grant leave to appeal on 9 February 2021, following the applicants having renewed their application to a full court. 3. During this period, the final confiscation hearing began in August 2018 but was postponed repeatedly due to a lack of court time. On 17 January 2020, over three years after the applicants’ conviction, the judge made a confiscation order against each applicant in the sum of £3,174,809.18, with a term of imprisonment in default fixed at ten years. 4. In his ruling, the Crown Court judge set out the following reasons for why the confiscation proceedings were delayed: • The finances “had been complicated”. Mistakes made by both parties’ financial experts resulted in several revisions of their reports. • The case was adjourned multiple times because the time estimate for the proceedings was inadequate, and counsel was unavailable for long periods of time. • Finally, following the final submissions, the judge was unable to issue his judgment for several months as he was given an insufficient number of reading days to consider and write it. 5. The applicants sought permission to appeal against the confiscation order, arguing that the hearing was unfair due to the delays. Permission to appeal was refused by both a Single Judge and, upon renewal, by the full Court of Appeal on 9 February 2021. The Court of Appeal held that the delays had not prejudiced the applicants. 6. The applicants then challenged the enforcement of the confiscation order in judicial review proceedings, again raising the issue of numerous delays. On 27 April 2022, the High Court refused permission, noting that the Court of Appeal had already found no prejudicial impact of the delays and that any alleged delay after the appeal decision had not been substantiated. The applicants were subsequently imprisoned for defaulting on payment of the confiscation order. 7. In their application to the ECtHR, the applicants relied on Article 6 § 1 of the Convention, alleging that the confiscation proceedings violated their right to a fair hearing within a reasonable time. 8. The ECtHR found that there had been a violation of Article 6 § 1 due to the excessive length of confiscation proceedings, which lasted four years and five months between the applicants’ conviction and the Court of Appeal’s refusal of permission to appeal. DH-DD(2026)385: Communication from the United Kingdom. Documents distributed at the request of a Representative shall be under the sole responsibility of the said Representative, without prejudice to the legal or political position of the Committee of Ministers. 9. The ECtHR held that although some delays were attributable to both parties, the “State was solely responsible for the unavailability of court days and the judge having insufficient reading days, which delayed his ruling for six months”. Having regard to what was at stake for the applicants during the proceedings (confiscation order for a large amount of money with a default term of ten years’ imprisonment), the ECtHR found that the “State was responsible for periods of delay which were unreasonably long and in breach of the reasonable time requirement” as provided by Article 6 § 1. The ECtHR found no violation of the fairness requirement under Article 6 § 1. INDIVIDUAL MEASURES Just satisfaction: 10. The applicants did not submit a claim for just satisfaction and no award was made. Other measures: 11. The Government considers that no individual measures are required. The Court found no violation of the fairness requirement under Article 6 § 1 of the Convention. While it did find a breach of the reasonable time requirement under Article 6 § 1, general measures are being taken to address timeliness issues in courts, as set out below. 12. Following the ECtHR’s ruling, one of the applicants (Mr Stefanos Neophytou) wrote to the Government requesting his immediate release from prison. The judgment does not require his release, and the Government does not intend to take any such action. This correspondence falls outside the scope of the execution process and is noted here solely for the Committee of Ministers’ information. 13. The applicants’ confiscation proceedings have come to an end. As such, there are no ongoing consequences of the violation that would necessitate further individual measures. GENERAL MEASURES 14. The delays in the applicants’ confiscation proceedings arose from exceptional, case-specific circumstances, including complex finances, unavailability of counsel, and a lack of court time and reading days. These issues were not systemic at the time the proceedings took place. However, pressures on the criminal courts have worsened delays in recent years which the Government is addressing through the general measures set out below. 15. His Majesty’s Court and Tribunals Service (HMCTS) support the efficient progression of cases by facilitating listings under the direction of the judiciary, who prioritise cases in the interests of justice, within available resources, and to meet statutory time limits. HMCTS actively manages case progression to ensure efficient use of time and enable the judiciary to list and hear cases effectively. Data on outstanding cases, including how long cases have been open, is reported regularly both at a national level to inform allocation of resources and locally to inform listing decisions. 16. The UK Government continues to ensure that appropriate procedures are in place to protect defendants and uphold fair trial safeguards throughout proceedings. This includes ensuring defendants have access to legal representation, that relevant evidence is disclosed by the prosecution in a timely manner, and that defendants are able to challenge the prosecution’s case, call witnesses, and present evidence. 17. The UK Government recognises the impact on defendants when trials are delayed. It actively monitors the timeliness of criminal cases and acknowledges that delays have increased in recent years. The Government has already taken measures to deliver swifter justice, including funding for criminal legal aid solicitor fees and significant investment in a record-high allocation of Crown Court sitting days to tackle the outstanding caseload - 111,250 in FY 25/26 and 2 DH-DD(2026)385: Communication from the United Kingdom. Documents distributed at the request of a Representative shall be under the sole responsibility of the said Representative, without prejudice to the legal or political position of the Committee of Ministers. uncapped sitting days in FY 26/27. Alongside this, the magistrates’ courts will be funded to its highest operational capacity. 18. To address rising demand in the criminal courts, the Government is also investing in long- term structural reform to the criminal courts system. It commissioned Sir Brian Leveson, former President of the Queen’s Bench Division and Head of Criminal Justice, to lead an independent review and propose ambitious reforms to improve timeliness in the courts. 19. Part 1 of Sir Brian Leveson’s Independent Review of the Criminal Courts, published on 9 July 2025, focused on ensuring that cases are dealt with proportionately. The Government has considered his recommendations for structural reform and is introducing many of his recommendations through legislation in the Courts and Tribunals Bill1 introduced in February 2026. 20. Part 2 of the Review, published on 4 February 2026, focused on maximising the efficiency of the criminal courts system. It included proposals for using Artificial Intelligence and other technologies to improve the performance of criminal courts which the Government will consider in due course. 21. These measures are expected to prevent similar violations by improving the efficiency and timeliness of criminal proceedings, reducing delays, and ensuring that defendants’ rights under Article 6 are fully protected. Publication: 22. The judgment has been published on the official database and summarised on websites, including: • HUDOC (23rd September 2025): https://hudoc.echr.coe.int/eng?i=001-244852 • No 5 Barristers Chambers (27th October 2025): Success for Tom Schofield KC in the ECHR - No5 Barristers' Chambers Dissemination: 23. The Government considers it unnecessary to disseminate the judgment because the delays in the applicant’s proceedings arose from exceptional, case-specific circumstances. The general measures outlined above are considered sufficient to address the breach of the reasonable time requirement under Article 6 § 1. State of execution of judgment: 24. The Government considers that all necessary individual and general measures have been taken, and the case should be closed. 1 Courts and Tribunals Bill - Parliamentary Bills - UK Parliament 3
